schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch map the whole chapter and tie the monthly courses to the census of 1 Chronicles 21; Ellicott reads v.1 as the heading, glossing 'courses' as military corps that came and went month by month, 24,000 strong.
Clarke reckons twelve captains each with a second over 24,000, an effective 300,000 at no cost to the state; Wesley notes the rotation was framed to keep the burden easy and equally shared.
Henry admires the prudent model of 24,000 changed monthly (288,000 in all) and turns it to the spiritual armour: readiness for war is the security of peace.
Gill fixes the subject as the militia, not the whole people, framed early in David's reign (Kimchi, Jarchi), as Asahel's case proves.

themes: order and dignity of the kingdom

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott identify Jashobeam son of Zabdiel, first captain of 24,000, with the chief of David's mighty men (1 Chronicles 11:11; 2 Samuel 23:8), noting all twelve generals stand in that earlier roll.
Gill glosses the first month as Nisan or Abib, answering to March.
Clarke supplies the Targum's names of the twelve months and notes no intercalary Veadar is reckoned.

Keil calls Jashobeam head of the army-chiefs for the first month; Ellicott fixes his descent from Perez (David's own branch) and weighs three senses of 'chief of all the captains,' careful not to trespass on Joab's place as commander-in-chief.

themes: Jashobeam of the branch of Perez

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott mend the captain's name from 1 Chronicles 11:12 to Eleazar/Eliezer son of Dodai the Ahohite, treat the Mikloth clause as textually corrupt (Bertheau's conjecture), and note Mikloth is a Benjamite name (1 Chronicles 8:32; 9:37).

themes: a corrupt line mended from parallels

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott lodge 'the priest' with the father Jehoiada and read 'head' as the predicate of Benaiah, commander of the third corps and hero of the thirty (1 Chronicles 11:22); Ellicott notes the LXX and Vulgate make Benaiah himself the priest.
Clarke reads kohen in its wider sense of prince or chief ruler, since Benaiah was never a priest, and distinguishes him from Benaiah the Pirathonite of the eleventh course.

debates.question: Does the text make Benaiah 'a chief priest,' or is the priesthood his father's and the title his own?

sides.position: 'The priest' belongs to Jehoiada the father; 'head' is Benaiah's, as commander of the third corps
kohen means prince or chief ruler here; no priesthood is meant, for Benaiah was never a priest

holders: the Septuagint and Vulgate (as reported by Ellicott)

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott dwell on Benaiah's eminence among the thirty (1 Chronicles 11:25; 2 Samuel 23:23) and read the clause on Ammizabad his son as parallel to the Mikloth clause, likely defective, the son standing as coadjutor or successor in the corps.

themes: a defective formula for the son

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil read 'Zebadiah his son after him' as pointing to Asahel's early death (slain by Abner, 2 Samuel 2:18): the corps was named for the fallen hero honoris causa while his son held command; Ellicott sees a key to the Mikloth and Ammizabad clauses.
Clarke argues the verse proves the whole arrangement was made before David reigned at Hebron, since Asahel was slain under Ishbosheth.

themes: named honoris causa after his death

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott reconcile Shamhuth with 'Shammoth' (1 Chronicles 11:27) and 'Shammah' (2 Samuel 23:25), read 'the Izrahite' as a slip for 'the Zarhite' (family of Zerah, 1 Chronicles 2:4), and fix his town as Harod.

themes: tracing a soldier by clan and town

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch identify Ira son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, captain of the sixth course, with the mighty man of 1 Chronicles 11:28 and 2 Samuel 23:26, anchoring the roster to the earlier roll of David's heroes.

Keil and Delitzsch place Helez the Pelonite, captain of the seventh course, by the parallels (1 Chronicles 11:27; 2 Samuel 23:26) and note he was 'of the children of Ephraim' — a northern tribe furnishing a commander to David's army.

Keil and Delitzsch place Sibbecai the Hushathite, captain of the eighth course, among the mighty men (1 Chronicles 11:29; 2 Samuel 23:27) and mark him a Zarhite, of the house of Zerah, like Shamhuth and Maharai.
Keil and Delitzsch place Abiezer the Anetothite, captain of the ninth course (1 Chronicles 11:28; 2 Samuel 23:27), fixing his town Anathoth in Benjamin (Jeremiah 1:1), the later home of Jeremiah.

themes: names rooted in the soil of Israel

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch place Maharai the Netophathite, captain of the tenth course (1 Chronicles 11:30; 2 Samuel 23:28), noting him a Zarhite of Zerah's house — the third such captain — his town Netophah near Bethlehem.

Keil and Delitzsch place Benaiah the Pirathonite, captain of the eleventh course (1 Chronicles 11:31; 2 Samuel 23:30), of Ephraim — a second Benaiah and second Ephraimite, distinct from Benaiah son of Jehoiada.

Keil corrects Heldai's name from the parallels (Heled, 1 Chronicles 11:30; Heleb, 2 Samuel 23:29) and fixes his clan of Othniel (Joshua 15:17); Ellicott glosses the name and tallies the generals' tribes — eight of Judah, two Ephraimite, one or two Benjamite.

themes: clan of Othniel, town of Netophah
the primacy of Judah and the breadth beyond it

schools.summary: Keil ties the princes' list to the army-list (internal business tribe by tribe) and notes Gad and Asher omitted 'for reasons unknown,' Levi doubled by Zadok; Ellicott adds that David took the war-leadership from the tribal emirs to himself.

Clarke supposes the account of Gad and Asher lost from the register, the princes honorary men like lords-lieutenant; Wesley (elsewhere) reads the princes as the king's chief counsellors, superior to the captains.
Gill distinguishes these hereditary tribal princes from David's appointed captains, and names Zadok, soon high priest, over the Aaronites as distinct from the Levites.

Henry surveys the whole: the ancient Mosaic order of tribal chiefs, Jaaziel son of Abner preferred (good for evil), the prideful census that 'turned to no good account,' and stewards all 'for service,' with David preferring his Bible to all his counsellors (Psalms 119:24).

debates.question: Why are Gad and Asher absent from the roll of tribal princes?

sides.position: The account of them was lost from the register

themes: princes ruling by right, not appointment
the ancient Mosaic order of tribal chiefs

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott note Levi's double headship — Hashabiah over the Levites and Zadok, the Nagid of Aaron and high priest of Eleazar's family (1 Chronicles 24:3), over the Aaronites — reading it as fitting the tribe's peculiar sanctity.

Keil and Ellicott identify Elihu, prince of Judah, with Eliab (1 Chronicles 2:13; LXX 'Eliab'), David's eldest brother and tribal prince by the firstborn's right (Ruth 4:17); Ellicott guesses Omri of Issachar an ancestor of the later king Omri (1 Kings 16:16).

themes: the firstborn's right and the youngest's crown

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch simply name Ishmaiah over Zebulun and Jerimoth over Naphtali, noting Zebulun (omitted in the genealogies of 1 Chronicles 4ff.) is here restored to the roll of princes — governance reaching the northern tribes.

themes: the northern tribes provided with heads

schools.summary: Keil names Hoshea over Ephraim and Joel over half-Manasseh, explaining the tribe's two princes by its division across the Jordan; Ellicott fixes this half as the western, between Ephraim and Issachar.

themes: two heads for a people split by the Jordan

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott place Iddo over eastern (Gilead-ward) Manasseh and Jaasiel son of Abner over Benjamin; Ellicott guesses Iddo an ancestor of the prophet Zechariah and Jaasiel a son of Saul's marshal Abner.

themes: the old rivalry of Saul's house healed

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott close the list with Azareel over Dan, note Dan and Zebulun restored while Gad and Asher are absent 'for reasons unknown,' the twelve made by counting Manasseh as two; Ellicott ties the order to Genesis 35:23 and doubts the idea that Dan is set last for its idolatry.
Wesley proposes Gad is omitted because that tribe was joined with the Reubenites under a single prince.

debates.question: Why does the roll omit Gad (and Asher)?

sides.position: Gad was joined with Reuben under one prince, so a separate head was needless
The reason cannot be said; the order likely just follows the compiler's source

themes: Dan and Zebulun restored, Gad and Asher absent

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the verse as a concluding note limiting the census (1 Chronicles 21) to those over twenty; David spared numbering all because to do so would distrust the promise to multiply Israel like the stars (Genesis 15:5; 22:17), and might not succeed.
Wesley: to number all, above and under twenty, would be an infinite trouble and a tempting of God, questioning the truth of his promise.
Gill, after Cornelius Bertram, reads David as having sinfully numbered robust youths under twenty, contrary to the law, and then suppressing the count from his chronicles to hide the transgression.

debates.question: Why did David not number those under twenty?

sides.position: An act of piety: to number all Israel would distrust and tempt God's promise to multiply them beyond counting
The verse conceals a sin: David wrongly numbered the robust under-aged and then suppressed the record

themes: the census restricted to military age
the promise of stars beyond number
the sin of the numbering recalled

schools.summary: Keil says Joab began but finished not, wrath falling, the number never entered in the chronicles; Ellicott sharpens the clauses (Joab omitted Levi and Benjamin, 1 Chronicles 21:6) and reads the unrecorded number as a clue that the chronicler drew on the royal annals.
Gill: Joab numbered by David's order but entirely against his own will.
Clarke: Joab did not return the whole number, the plague having begun before he finished, or from his own reluctance to tell the king how numerous the people were.

debates.question: Why was Joab's census left unfinished and never entered in the annals?

sides.position: Wrath fell for the sin, and David would not own or record a count got by transgression
The plague broke out before Joab finished, or he withheld the number from his own reluctance

themes: the number struck from the record
a window on the chronicler's sources

schools.summary: Gill runs down the stewards in order — Azmaveth over the treasures, Jehonathan over the country storehouses, Ezri over the tillage, Shimei over the vineyards, Zabdi over the wine, Baalhanan over the olives — each domain with its overseer.
Clarke inventories the king's substance: treasures, goods and grain, vineyards, olives, cattle, camels and asses (no horses), and flocks — the wealth of a landed prince in a simple age.

Ellicott fixes the terms: the treasures were those of the palace on Zion, the 'storehouses' (same word) the country holdings in fields, cities, villages, and towers; Jehonathan was comptroller-general of those revenues.

themes: treasures on Zion and produce in the country

schools.summary: Keil sets Ezri son of Chelub over the field-labourers who tilled the royal ground, distinct from the officers of vineyards and wine that follow; Ellicott calls him steward of the arable domains — the husbandry finely graded, each stage under its own head.

themes: the kingdom ordered to the furrow

schools.summary: Ellicott fixes Shimei of Ramah in Benjamin (Joshua 18:25) over the vineyards and Zabdi (Zebadiah) of Shiphmoth (1 Samuel 30:28) over the wine-cellars, recalling Judah's fame as a wine-land (Genesis 49:11; the grapes of Eshcol, Numbers 13:23).
Wesley marks the deliberate division: one officer over the vineyard-labourers, another over the fruit, as one was over the field-workers and another over the stored produce.

themes: tending and keeping in separate hands

schools.summary: Keil sets Baalhanan the Gederite over the olives and sycamores of the shephelah and Joash over the oil-stores; Ellicott describes the fig-mulberry (its figs the food of the poor, Amos 7:14) and reads Baal-hanan, 'the Lord bestowed,' as a quiet doxology.

themes: olives and sycamores of the lowland

schools.summary: Keil names Shitrai over the herds in Sharon and Shaphat over the herds in the valleys; Ellicott fixes Sharon as the coastal pasture-strip between Caesarea and Joppa (Isaiah 33:9), the valleys as the Judean highlands — the herds parcelled region by region.

themes: herds of Sharon and the valleys

schools.summary: Ellicott notes Obil's name means 'camel-keeper' in Arabic and that he was an Ishmaelite, fittest to tend the desert beast; the 'asses' are she-asses (Genesis 49:14; Zechariah 9:9), and Jehdeiah's Meronoth a town perhaps near Mizpah.

Ellicott sets Jaziz the Hagerite over the flocks (the Hagrim conquered in East Gilead, 1 Chronicles 5:10), reads 'rulers of the substance' as princes over the movable wealth (Genesis 12:5) now grown to husbandry and commerce (1 Chronicles 22:4); Keil closes the stewards and turns to the councillors.

Wesley: all these officers are for substance, none for state or sport — David a great soldier, scholar, and prince, yet a great husband of his estate, and magistrates who would have industrious subjects must themselves be examples of application to business.

themes: David the great husband of his estate

schools.summary: Keil frames the list as David's first councillors — a senate, companion to the earlier officer-lists (1 Chronicles 18:15; 2 Samuel 8:15) — headed by Jonathan the king's uncle, a wise scribe, with Jehiel son of Hachmoni set with the king's sons.
Wesley glosses 'scribe' as one learned in the law of God (also the law of the land) or the king's secretary, and Jehiel's charge 'with the king's sons' as their tutor or governor.

themes: a tutor for the king's sons

schools.summary: Keil identifies Ahithophel the king's counsellor, whose counsel was an oracle and who hanged himself when spurned (2 Samuel 16:23; 17:23), and Hushai the Archite the king's companion, who defeated Ahithophel's counsel in Absalom's revolt (2 Samuel 15:37; 16:16).

Wesley distinguishes the counsellor (whose counsel the king most prized) from the companion or friend (trusted with his secrets), and moralises: 'A cunning man was his counsellor, but an honest man was his friend.'

Keil reads 'after Ahithophel' as after his death: Jehoiada son of Benaiah (named for his grandfather) and Abiathar the high priest succeed as counsellors, while Joab remains general of the host (1 Chronicles 18:15).

Wesley marks these as chief counsellors once Ahithophel was gone; Clarke passes on the Targum's scene of inquiry by Urim and Thummim and crowns the chapter with the moral that nothing went by favour — each man set over the office he was best qualified for.

outliers.note: Passes on the Targum's anachronistic embellishment — inquiry by Urim and Thummim, Jehoiada as prince of the Sanhedrin — which reads a later institution back into David's day, though it preserves the true order of counsel-then-command.

themes: the succession of counsellors after Ahithophel
nothing by favour, all by fitness
a realm ordered by wisdom and worth
